scenario: A Java application uses Spring Zeebe and hosts many workers handling many small jobs. The team observes underutilisation -- the workers seem to wait for jobs instead of running them concurrently. They want intra-JVM parallelism -- multiple threads handling jobs concurrently within one process.
question: Which Spring Zeebe configuration tunes intra-JVM concurrency?

options.text: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter is fundamentally single-threaded within a JVM, and achieving parallelism requires spawning multiple JVM processes or Docker containers, each running a separate Spring Boot instance. This claim is inaccurate. md), allowing multiple worker threads to process activated jobs concurrently within a single JVM. The default value of 1 thread creates the false impression of single-threaded-only operation, but increasing this value enables intra-JVM parallelism without requiring additional JVM instances.

Spawn multiple JVM instances (Docker containers) to achieve parallelism, distributing the job workload across multiple separate processes -- each container subscribes to the same task types and receives an equal share of activated jobs from Zeebe. While horizontal scaling is a valid production strategy for handling high job volumes, it addresses a different concern than the question asks: intra-JVM parallelism means improving utilisation within a single running process. Multiple containers without intra-JVM tuning leave each container underutilised. Both strategies are complementary, but the question specifically targets the intra-JVM configuration.

max jobs active (or per worker override) so the threads have jobs to process (per :780 795). The thread pool processes activated jobs concurrently; max jobs active controls how many jobs each worker holds at once. Note: the docs caution at :722 724 that the team generally recommends async code over a large thread pool -- see "writing good workers" guidance before scaling threads aggressively.

The Camunda Spring Boot Starter has no configurable concurrency parameters -- worker execution is entirely self-managed by the framework and teams cannot tune thread counts or job activation limits. This claim is inaccurate. max jobs active (maximum concurrent jobs per worker subscription), and per-worker timeout settings. The configuration documentation explicitly covers these properties and provides guidance on appropriate values for different workload characteristics.

a.text: Incorrect. Intra-JVM parallelism in the Camunda Spring Boot starter is controlled by two related knobs: (1) executor thread pool (camunda.client.execution-threads, default 1) -- how many threads in the JVM process activated jobs in parallel; (2) camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active (or per-worker override) -- how many jobs each worker subscription activates at a time. For full utilisation, threads × workers ≥ summed max-jobs-active. Tune based on workload: CPU-bound → match thread count to CPU cores; I/O-bound → consider whether async code is a better fit than a wider pool (per the doc's note at configuration.md:722-724). Note on Pattern #18: the legacy spring-zeebe-sdk artifact is being removed in Camunda 8.10 -- replacement is the Camunda Spring Boot starter at apis-tools/camunda-spring-boot-starter/. Any doc link to apis-tools/spring-zeebe-sdk/... is stale.

b.text: Incorrect. The Camunda Spring Boot starter is multi-threaded; default execution-threads is 1, but configurable.

c.text: Correct. Multiple JVMs adds horizontal scaling but doesn't address intra-JVM efficiency. Best to tune both.

explanation: Multiple JVMs adds horizontal scaling but doesn't address intra-JVM efficiency. Best to tune both.
